title: Considerations on Representative Government
description: Considerations on Representative Government — knowledge fragments for AI agents

memes.label: About This Wiki
memes.description: A knowledge wiki for "代议制政府" — this page is auto-generated from book metadata

memes.label: 'Table of Contents'
memes.description: 'Table of Contents'

memes.label: American Constitution
memes.description: The codified federal Constitution of the United States, analyzed by John Stuart Mill as a case study in the separation of powers, federal judicial review, and the design of representative institutions. (017-006)

memes.label: Aristocracy
memes.description: A form of government or social class in which political power is held by a privileged hereditary or wealth-based elite — Mill's comparative analysis of its merits and defects as a governing institution.

memes.label: Athenian Democracy
memes.description: Mill's principal classical reference point — the direct democracy of ancient Athens, analyzed as both a model of citizen participation and a cautionary example throughout Considerations on Representative Government.

memes.label: Australia
memes.description: Mill's reference to the Australian colonies as examples of dependencies populated by settlers of European origin, capable of representative self-government. (018-003)

memes.label: Austria
memes.description: Mill's comparative reference to Austria as an example of feudal monarchy, bureaucratic government, and multinational empire — contrasted unfavorably with free states and representative institutions. (003-013)

memes.label: Ballot
memes.description: The method of casting votes in political elections — Mill's controversial argument that voting should ordinarily be open rather than secret, treating the suffrage as a public trust rather than a private right.

memes.label: bicameralism
memes.description: Redirect to [[second-chamber]].

memes.label: British Constitution
memes.description: The unwritten constitutional framework of Great Britain, analyzed by John Stuart Mill as an exemplar of representative government where unwritten maxims and political morality balance formal legal powers. (005-002)

memes.label: Bureaucracy
memes.description: Government by a permanent professional administrative class — Mill's comparative analysis of bureaucracy as a rival both to representative democracy and to traditional aristocracy, its unique strengths as an administrative system, and its fatal defect as a governing institution.

memes.label: Cabinet
memes.description: The executive council of ministers responsible for administration and policy — analyzed by Mill as the body that should act as a Commission of Legislation rather than exercising ultimate political control (005-013).

memes.label: Canada
memes.description: Referenced by Mill in Ch.18 as a successful example of colonial self-government — a dependency granted complete internal self-rule while remaining under imperial authority for external affairs (018-006).

memes.description: "Of the Proper Functions of Representative Bodies."

memes.description: "Of the Infirmities and Dangers to which Representative"

memes.description: "Ought Pledges to be Required from Members of Parliament?"

memes.description: "Of a Second Chamber."

memes.label: "Chapter 14: Of the Executive in a Representative Government."
memes.description: "Of the Executive in a Representative Government."

memes.label: "Chapter 15: Of Local Representative Bodies."
memes.description: "Of Local Representative Bodies."

memes.label: "Chapter 16: Of Nationality, as connected with Representative"
memes.description: "Of Nationality, as connected with Representative"

memes.description: "Of Federal Representative Governments."

memes.label: China
memes.description: Mill's key comparative example of a civilization whose "paternal despotism" successfully carried it to a certain point of development but then became a barrier to further progress by suppressing mental liberty and individuality (002-029).

memes.label: Civil Service
memes.description: The permanent, professional body of public servants who conduct the detail of government business under ministerial control, recruited through competitive examination and protected from arbitrary dismissal in Mill's theory of representative government (014-015).

memes.label: Civilization
memes.description: The progressive advancement of a society in knowledge, institutions, and collective capacity for self-government — a precondition for representative institutions in Mill's theory.

memes.label: Class Interest
memes.description: The tendency of any social class to govern in its own exclusive interest rather than the common good — Mill's analysis of why class-based domination is the central danger to representative government and how institutions must be designed to neutralize it.

memes.label: Class Legislation
memes.description: The danger that a representative body will enact laws favoring the interests

memes.label: Samuel Taylor Coleridge
memes.description: English poet and philosopher whose distinction between "Permanence" and "Progression" Mill critiques in Ch.2 as an inadequate basis for a philosophy of government (002-004).

memes.label: Colonies
memes.description: Territories governed by a distant metropolitan power — Mill's analysis

memes.label: Competitive Examination
memes.description: The principle, advocated by Mill for entry into the civil service, that public appointments should be awarded to those who most distinguish themselves in open, competitive tests administered by non-political examiners, rather than by patronage or mere pass-level qualification (014-016).

memes.label: Confederation
memes.description: A union of sovereign states under a common government for limited purposes, discussed by Mill in the context of federal representative governments and their advantages over complete separation or full consolidation.

memes.label: Confidence
memes.description: The trust that citizens place in their governors and institutions — Mill's analysis of confidence as a condition of effective government, the mechanisms by which it is earned and lost, and its relationship to representative institutions.

memes.label: Consent
memes.description: The principle that legitimate government rests on the consent of the governed — Mill's nuanced treatment of consent as both a foundation of free government and a concept requiring careful institutional specification.

memes.label: Constituency
memes.description: The geographical unit of electoral representation — Mill's critique of constituency-based elections and his argument for a system in which voters may choose representatives beyond their local district through proportional representation.

memes.label: Constituent
memes.description: An elector who is a member of a representative's constituency — Mill analyzes the proper relationship between constituents and their representatives, rejecting the view that constituents should give binding instructions (012-001).

relations.label: reduce their representative into a delegate

memes.label: Constitution
memes.description: The fundamental framework of government — its design principles, distribution of powers, and role in securing good government as analyzed by John Stuart Mill.

memes.label: Constitutional Morality
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's concept of the unwritten ethical norms and customary restraints that sustain constitutional government by limiting the exercise of formal legal powers (005-003).

memes.label: Council
memes.description: In Mill's political theory, a consultative body of expert advisers attached to executive ministers, designed to combine individual responsibility with collective wisdom, and distinguished from decision-making boards by its purely advisory function (014-006).

memes.label: County Board
memes.description: A form of local representative body at the county level, discussed by John Stuart Mill in his treatment of local government as a school of political capacity and an alternative to aristocratic administration. (015-005)

memes.label: Delegate
memes.description: A conception of the representative as an agent bound to carry out the instructions of constituents — rejected by Mill in favor of the independent trustee model (012-001).

memes.label: Deliberation
memes.description: The process of careful consideration and discussion of public affairs, identified by Mill as the defining function of representative assemblies (005-008).

memes.label: Democracy
memes.description: The form of government in which the people, or a numerous portion of them, exercise the controlling power — its varieties, defects, and the reforms Mill proposed to make it a government of intelligence rather than mediocrity.

memes.label: Dependencies
memes.description: Territories subject to the authority of a free state without full participation in its representative institutions — a key concept in Mill's Ch.18 on imperial governance (018-001).

memes.label: Dependency
memes.description: A territory or population governed by a foreign power without full participation in its own government — Mill's analysis of the distinctive challenges of governing dependencies and the structural defects of direct imperial administration.

memes.label: Despotism
memes.description: Government by absolute rule without legal or institutional checks — Mill's critique of despotism as incompatible with the development of public intelligence, even in its most benevolent form.

memes.label: Benjamin Disraeli
memes.description: British Conservative statesman and author (1804–1881), twice Prime Minister, referenced by John Stuart Mill in Considerations on Representative Government in connection with electoral reform debates and Conservative political principles. (007-007)

memes.label: Dissolution of Parliament
memes.description: The executive power to dissolve the representative assembly and call a new election, which Mill regards as an essential constitutional mechanism for resolving deadlocks, testing public opinion, and maintaining executive independence without resort to fixed terms or popular election of the chief magistrate (014-010).

memes.label: East India Company
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's analysis of the East India Company as a case study in the challenges of bureaucratic governance and accountability in dependent territories.

memes.label: Education
memes.description: The cultivation of intelligence and public spirit through political participation — Mill's theory of education as the primary function of free government and the ultimate test of its success.

memes.label: Election
memes.description: The process by which citizens choose their representatives — its methods, defects, and the reforms John Stuart Mill proposed to improve the intellectual quality of elected bodies.

memes.label: Equality
memes.description: The principle that all citizens should have equal standing in the political process — Mill's analysis of the conditions under which equality is desirable, attainable, and consistent with good government.

memes.label: Executive
memes.description: The branch of government responsible for implementing laws and administering public affairs — its proper constitution, powers, and relationship to the legislature as analyzed by John Stuart Mill.

memes.label: Federal Government
memes.description: A system in which separate political communities are united under a common government for certain purposes while retaining distinct governments for others — analyzed by Mill as a stable alternative to complete unification.

memes.label: The Federalist
memes.description: A collection of papers by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay written in defense of the proposed United States Constitution — described by Mill as the most instructive treatise on federal government (017-005).

memes.label: France
memes.description: France as analyzed by Mill — a case study in centralized administration, aristocratic monarchy, and national homogeneity, serving as a comparative reference throughout Considerations on Representative Government.

memes.label: Franchise
memes.description: The right to vote in elections for representative bodies — Mill's analysis of who should possess the franchise, on what terms, and with what institutional safeguards against abuse.

memes.label: Free Government
memes.description: A form of government in which citizens participate in their own governance

memes.label: Freedom
memes.description: The positive capacity for collective self-direction — Mill's conception of freedom as the practice of governing oneself through institutions that develop rather than suppress the active faculties of citizens.

memes.label: Germany
memes.description: Mill's reference to the German Confederation as the archetypal weak federal system — a league of governments rather than a government of citizens — contrasted with the American federal model. (017-005)

memes.label: Government
memes.description: The system or form by which a state or community is governed; in Mill's analysis, a matter of choice rather than either pure mechanical invention or organic growth, but always subject to the conditions of the people for whom it is intended. (001-001)

memes.label: Governor
memes.description: A professional ruler or executive officer who conducts the work of government; in Mill's analysis, governors must be selected, watched, and controlled by the representative body that acts on behalf of the governed. (006-008)

memes.label: Great Britain
memes.description: Mill's primary model and reference point for representative government throughout Considerations on Representative Government, used extensively as the empirical basis for his institutional analysis and comparative judgments.

memes.label: Greece
memes.description: Mill's dual use of Greece as both the classical model of civic excellence in ancient city-states and a cautionary modern example of a people unready for representative institutions. (003-003)

memes.label: House of Commons
memes.description: The House of Commons as analyzed by John Stuart Mill — the elected chamber of Parliament whose composition, functions, and reform constitute the institutional heart of representative government.

memes.label: House of Lords
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's analysis of the House of Lords as a second chamber — a transitional institution whose aristocratic character is increasingly at odds with democratic principles.

memes.label: British Governance in India
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's analysis of British governance in India as a case study in the challenges of administering dependent territories without representative institutions.

memes.label: Indirect Election
memes.description: A system in which voters choose intermediate electors who in turn select the members of the representative assembly, rather than voting for representatives directly (009-001).

memes.label: Intelligence (Political)
memes.description: The capacity for reasoned judgment on public affairs — Mill's analysis of intelligence as a fundamental element of good government, the condition for its exercise, and the quality that representative institutions must cultivate in the citizenry.

memes.label: Italy
memes.description: Mill's references to Italy — the medieval Italian republics as examples of free civic communities, and the 19th-century Risorgimento as a case of legitimate national unification through federal union. (003-013)

memes.label: james-mill
memes.description: Redirect to [[jeremy-bentham]].

memes.label: Jeremy Bentham
memes.description: English philosopher (1748–1832), founder of modern utilitarianism; his

memes.label: Jesuits
memes.description: Catholic religious order whose Paraguay missions Mill cites as an example of "government of leading-strings" — a paternalistic governance model suited to populations not yet ready for representative institutions (002-029).

memes.label: John Stuart Mill
memes.description: Author of Considerations on Representative Government (1861), British

memes.label: judicial-review
memes.description: Redirect to [[supreme-court]].

memes.label: Jury
memes.description: The institution of trial by jury — its role in Mill's theory as a mechanism of popular participation in government, its educative function for citizens, and its place within the broader system of free institutions.

memes.label: Legislation
memes.description: The function of making laws — one of the primary responsibilities of representative bodies, distinct from administration and requiring specific qualifications and institutional safeguards.

memes.label: Legislative Commission
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's proposal for a permanent, expert body responsible for drafting laws, forming a key element of his vision for a well-constituted representative government. (005-013)

memes.label: Liberty
memes.description: The condition of being free from arbitrary constraint — Mill's analysis of liberty as both the end of government and the means by which citizens develop the capacities necessary for self-rule.

memes.label: Majority
memes.description: The numerical preponderance that, in democratic theory, entitles a group to govern; Mill demonstrates that simple majority rule, without safeguards for minority representation, can produce government by a minority of the nation. (007-004)

memes.label: Mandate
memes.description: The authority granted to representatives by electors for a defined term — discussed by Mill in relation to the duration of parliaments and the independence of representatives from constituent instructions (011-002).

memes.label: Minority
memes.description: The political minority — those outnumbered in the electoral system — and why their representation is, for John Stuart Mill, an essential test of democratic legitimacy.

memes.label: Monarchy
memes.description: Rule by one — Mill's comparative analysis of absolute monarchy versus representative government, the conditions under which monarchy may be suitable, and its inherent limitations as a form of government for civilized peoples.

memes.label: Municipal Government
memes.description: Local self-government at the town and county level — Mill's analysis of municipal institutions as both an administrative necessity and the primary school of public spirit in a free state.

memes.label: Nationality
memes.description: The sentiment of common identity uniting a people through shared sympathies of race, language, religion, geography, and political history; Mill argues that free institutions are nearly impossible in multi-national states (016-001).

memes.label: Oligarchy
memes.description: A form of government in which power is concentrated in the hands of a small ruling elite; discussed by Mill as a recurring danger in both aristocratic and democratic systems (006-007).

memes.label: Opposition
memes.description: The organised political force that opposes the government of the day — Mill's analysis of the role of opposition in checking power, testing policy, and ensuring that government remains accountable to the represented.

memes.label: Parish Relief
memes.description: The system of welfare support for the destitute which, in Mill's view, should disqualify recipients from the electoral franchise on the principle that economic dependency is incompatible with full political citizenship (008-006).

memes.label: Parliament
memes.description: The supreme legislative body in representative government — its role, functions, and relationship to the executive as analyzed by John Stuart Mill in *Considerations on Representative Government*.

memes.label: Participation
memes.description: The active engagement of citizens in public affairs — Mill's argument that participation is both a safeguard of individual interests and the primary mechanism of political education.

memes.label: Party
memes.description: Organized political groups that compete for control of representative institutions — Mill's analysis of their inevitability, functions, and dangers to deliberative government.

memes.label: Patriotism
memes.description: Devotion to the public good and the obligations of citizenship; Mill regards patriotism as a national quality that is "the fruit solely of freedom" and essential for preventing national decline (003-003).

memes.label: Plato
memes.description: Ancient Greek philosopher referenced by John Stuart Mill in Considerations on Representative Government for his views on political competence and the duty of governance.

memes.label: Pledge
memes.description: A commitment made by a candidate to electors to support specific measures if elected — analyzed by Mill in Chapter XII as a constraint on the independence of representatives (012-003).

memes.label: Pledges
memes.description: The practice of parliamentary candidates binding themselves by pledges to their constituents, and its implications for representative government (012-003).

memes.label: Plural Voting
memes.description: A system proposed by John Stuart Mill in which citizens with superior education or occupational attainment receive multiple votes, giving their opinions greater weight than those of less educated voters (008-010).

memes.label: Plurality (of Votes)
memes.description: The practice of assigning more than one vote to certain electors on the basis of superior qualifications — Mill's carefully qualified defence of plurality as a mechanism for securing intelligence in the representative body.

memes.label: Political Institution
memes.description: The formal structures through which political power is organized and exercised — for Mill, institutions are neither wholly invented nor wholly organic, but must be evaluated by their effects on the moral and intellectual advancement of the people.

memes.label: Polity
memes.description: The organised political community as a whole — Mill's use of "polity" to denote the people, institutions, and political culture that together constitute a self-governing society.

memes.label: "Preface"
memes.description: "Preface to Considerations on Representative Government"

memes.label: Progress
memes.description: The onward movement of society in wealth, knowledge, institutions, and moral capacity — a central concept in Mill's theory of historical development and the criterion for evaluating forms of government.

memes.label: Property
memes.description: Property as a basis for political rights and a qualification for the franchise — Mill's analysis of the relationship between property ownership, political competence, and the structure of representative government.

memes.label: Proportional Representation
memes.description: An electoral system in which each division of the electoral body receives representation in proportion to its numerical strength, advocated by Mill as the essential mechanism for ensuring that minorities are heard in the representative assembly (008-014).

memes.label: Prussia
memes.description: Historical German kingdom referenced by Mill as an example in discussions of federal government and national unity, particularly regarding territorial contiguity and military intervention in federal systems (016-004).

memes.label: Public Office
memes.description: The position of formal authority in the apparatus of government — Mill's analysis of how public offices should be constituted, the principle of responsibility, and the distinction between policy-making offices and subordinate administrative posts.

memes.label: Public Opinion
memes.description: The collective judgment of the citizenry — its formation, its role in checking government, and its dependence on publicity, discussion, and education in Mill's theory of representative government.

memes.label: Reform Bill 1832
memes.description: The Representation of the People Act 1832 — a landmark reform of the British electoral system that expanded the franchise and redistributed parliamentary seats, frequently referenced and analyzed by John Stuart Mill.

memes.label: Reform of Parliament
memes.description: The broader movement for parliamentary reform in 19th-century Britain — its debates, conflicting creeds, and implications for representative government as analyzed by John Stuart Mill.

memes.label: Representation
memes.description: The mechanism by which the people delegate political power to elected members — its principles, forms, and defects as analyzed by John Stuart Mill.

memes.label: Representative Democracy
memes.description: The form of democracy in which citizens govern through elected deputies — Mill's preferred model, its institutional requirements, and its distinction from both bureaucracy and the "false democracy" of local majorities.

memes.label: Representative Government
memes.description: A form of government in which the whole people or a numerous portion

memes.label: Republic
memes.description: A form of government without a monarch, in which the head of state is elected or appointed — discussed by Mill in the context of ancient and modern republics, the executive power, and the ballot.

memes.label: Responsibility
memes.description: The principle, central to Mill's theory of representative government, that every executive function should be the appointed duty of a specific individual who bears undivided accountability for its outcomes (014-002).

memes.label: responsible-government
memes.description: Redirect to [[executive]].

memes.label: Rights
memes.description: The entitlements of individuals in a political community — Mill's functional argument for political rights as protections of interest, instruments of education, and foundations of representative government.

memes.label: Roman Republic
memes.description: The Roman Republic as Mill's classical example of aristocratic governance — its Senate, dual executive, and the conditions that enabled representative character despite formal oligarchy.

memes.label: rotten-boroughs
memes.description: Redirect to [[reform-bill-1832]].

memes.label: Second Chamber
memes.description: The upper house in a bicameral legislature — analyzed by Mill as a moderating check on democratic ascendancy, with preferred models based on expertise rather than hereditary privilege.

memes.label: Self-Government
memes.description: The capacity of a people to govern themselves through their own representative institutions — Mill's analysis of self-government as both the end of political development and the practice through which citizens acquire the virtues necessary for freedom.

memes.label: Senate
memes.description: A governing or legislative body typically composed of experienced public officials and statesmen; in Mill's analysis, the Roman Senate and the United States Senate serve as models for how a well-composed second chamber can supply the special training and knowledge that popular assemblies lack. (006-007)

memes.label: Sinister Interest
memes.description: Bentham's term for interests that conflict with the general good — Mill's analysis of how sinister interests operate in monarchy, aristocracy, and democracy, and why institutional checks are necessary in every form of government.

memes.label: Slavery
memes.description: The institution of personal bondage, discussed by Mill as a historical stage in civilizational development, a metaphor for political subjection, and a moral wrong abolished through the progress of opinion.

memes.label: Sovereignty
memes.description: The supreme controlling power in the last resort; Mill argues that in the ideally best government, sovereignty is vested in the entire aggregate of the community, with every citizen having a voice in its exercise (003-007).

memes.label: Suffrage
memes.description: The right to vote in political elections — Mill's analysis of who should possess it, how it should be weighted, and why universal suffrage alone is insufficient for good government.

memes.label: Supreme Court
memes.description: The highest judicial tribunal in a federal system, serving as independent umpire between federal and state governments with the power of constitutional review (017-006).

memes.label: Switzerland
memes.description: Switzerland as Mill's European case study in federalism — its pre-1847 confederation model, the transition to effective federal government, and its lessons for multinational states.

memes.label: Thomas Hare
memes.description: British political reformer (1806–1891) and inventor of the Hare system

memes.label: Alexis de Tocqueville
memes.description: French political thinker and author of Democracy in America, cited by Mill as a major authority on the connection between democratic institutions and the cultivation of public intelligence (008-002).

memes.label: trustee
memes.description: Redirect to [[delegate]].

memes.label: Tyranny
memes.description: The abuse of political power for private or factional ends — Mill's analysis of tyranny in its traditional form and its modern democratic variant, the tyranny of the majority.

memes.label: United States
memes.description: The United States as Mill's primary comparative case study — its federal constitution, electoral system, and democratic institutions examined throughout Considerations on Representative Government.

memes.label: Universal Suffrage
memes.description: The principle that every adult citizen should possess the right to vote — Mill's qualified endorsement and the institutional safeguards he considered necessary to prevent universal suffrage from degrading the quality of government.

memes.label: university-representation
memes.description: Redirect to [[house-of-commons]].

memes.label: Utility
memes.description: The ethical principle that actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, serving as the foundational moral standard for Mill's political philosophy.

memes.label: Virtue
memes.description: The moral and intellectual qualities of the people that Mill identifies as the foundational element of good government and the primary object of political institutions to promote (002-020).

memes.label: Voting
memes.description: The act and right of casting a ballot — its justifications, limits, and Mill's argument for weighted plural voting as a transitional reform.

memes.label: Women's Suffrage
memes.description: John Stuart Mill's argument that women should be granted the right to vote on equal terms with men, presented as an integral part of his case for representative government (008-016).
